Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - CONVERGENCE OF GENERALIZED BELIEF PROPAGA TION ALGORITHM ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Belief propagation is a fundamental message-passing algorithm for numerous applications in machine learning. It is known that belief propagation algorithm is exact on tree graphs. However, belief propagation is run on loopy graphs in most applications. So, understanding the behavior of belief propagation on loopy graphs has been a major topic for researchers in different areas. In this paper, we study the convergence behavior of generalized belief propagation algorithm on graphs with motifs (triangles, loops, etc.) We show under a certain initialization, generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Undirected graphical models, also known as Markov Random Fields (MRF), provide a framework for modeling high dimensional distributions with dependent variables. Ising models are a special class of discrete pairwise graphical models originated from statistical physics. Ising models have numerous applications in computer vision Ravikumar et al. (2010), bio-informatics Marbach et al. (2012), and social networks Eagle et al. (2009). Explicitly, the joint distribution of Ising model is given by

where _{Xi}i ∈{±_ 1 _}[n]_ are random variables valued in a binary alphabet (also known as "spins"), _Jij_ represents the pairwise interactions between spin _i_ and spin _j_ , _hi_ represents the external field for spin _i_ , _β_ = 1 _/T_ is the reciprocal temperature, and _Z_ is a normalization constant called _partition function_ .

Historically, Ising models are proposed to study ferromagnetism. However, researchers find the computational complexity is the main challenge of performing sampling and inference on Ising models. In the literature, there are multiple ways to tackle the computational complexity. One of the ways are Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms. A well-known example is Gibbs sampling, which is a special case of the Metropolis–Hastings algorithm. Basically Gibbs sampling samples a random variable conditioned on the distribution based on the previous samples. It can be shown that Gibbs sampling generates a reversible Markov chain of samples. Thus, the stationary distribution of the Markov chain is the desired joint distribution over the random variables, and it can be reached after the _burn-in period_ . However, it is also well-known that Gibbs sampling will become trapped on multi-modal distribution. For example, Smith and Roberts (1993) and Mengersen (1996) show that when the joint distribution is bi-modal, the Gibbs sampling iterations may be trapped in one of the modes, reducing the probability of convergence.

Another popular way to go around the computational complexity is _variational methods_ , which makes some approximation to the joint distribution. These methods usually turn the inference problem with respect to the approximate joint distribution into some non-convex optimization problem, and solve it either by the standard optimization methods, e.g, gradient descent, or by specialized algorithms like belief propagation. However, due to the non-convexity, those methods usually do not have theoretical guarantees that the solution converges to the global optimum.

Belief propagation (BP) is an effective numerical method for solving inference problems on graphical models. It was originally proposed by Pearl (2014) for tree-like graphs. Ever since it plays a fundamental role in numerous applications including coding theory Frey et al. (1998); Richardson

and Urbanke (2001), constraint satisfaction problems Achlioptas and Moore (2006), and community detection in the stochastic block model Decelle et al. (2011). It is well-known that belief propagation is only exact for a model on a graph with locally tree-like structures. The long haunting question is, theoretically how does belief propagation perform on loopy graphs.

We now describe the related work and our contributions.

## **Related work and our contribution**

In a classic work, Yedidia et al. (2003) establishes the connection between belief propagation and the Bethe free energy. He shows that there is one-to-one correspondence between the fixed points of belief propagation and stationary points of the Bethe free energy. Following his work, it is known that the Bethe free energy at the critical points can be represented in terms of fixed point messages of belief propagation Montanari (2013). In a recent work, Koehler (2019) further studies the properties of Bethe free energy at the critical points, and shows for ferromagnetic Ising models, initialized with all one messages, belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy. However, those theories consider either asymptotic locally tree-like graphs, or loopy graphs with simple edges. Real technological, social and biological networks have numerous short and large loops and other complex motifs, which lead to non-tree-like structures and essentially loopy graphs with hyper edges. Newman Newman (2009); Karrer and Newman (2010) and Miller (2009) independently propose a model of random graphs with arbitrary distributions of motifs. And Yoon et al. (2011) generalizes the Belief Propagation to graphs with motifs.

Our work builds on generalized belief propagation on graphs with motifs Yoon et al. (2011) and the convergence of belief propagation on ferromagnetic Ising models on loopy graphs with simple edges Koehler (2019). In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.

## 2 ISING MODELS ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS

Let us introduce the concept of graphs with motifs. In graphs with motifs, each vertex belongs to a given set of motifs. As shown in Fig.1a , different motifs can be attached to vertex _i_ : a simple edge ( _i, j_ ), a triangle, a square, a pentagon, and other non-clique motifs. Graphs with motifs can be viewed as hyper-graphs where motifs play a role of hyper-edges. And the number of specific motifs attached to a vertex is equal to hyper-degree with respect to the specific motifs. In this paper, for simplicity, we only consider simple motifs such as simple edges, and cliques.

Consider the Ising model with arbitrary order of interactions among vertices in each motif on a hyper-graph. Let _Ml_ ( _i_ ) denote a cluster of size _l_ attached to vertex _i_ , where vertices _j_ 1 _, j_ 2 _, . . . , jl−_ 1 together with _i_ form the motif. And let _X_ denote the random variable of spin configurations, the Hamiltonian of the model is

As discussed in the previous section, most previous works focus on Ising models with pairwise interactions. In this paper, we are interested in Ising models with higher order interactions. For simplicity, we consider Ising models with only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles. Our derivation can be extended to more general cases.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper investigates the convergence properties of generalized belief propagation (GBP) on graphs with motifs—such as triangles and higher-order cliques—for ferromagnetic Ising models, extending prior theoretical results that have largely focused on locally tree-like or simple loopy graphs. The work is well-motivated, clearly situated within the relevant literature, and addresses an important gap in the theoretical understanding of message-passing algorithms in structured, non-tree-like networks arising in real-world domains. The writing is generally clear, and the progression from problem motivation to technical setup is logical. A key strength lies in its principled extension of convergence guarantees from standard belief propagation (as established by Koehler, 2019) to the more complex setting of generalized BP on hyper-graphs with motifs. However, the most significant issue is the incomplete development of the theoretical argument: while the paper claims convergence of GBP to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy under all-ones initialization, it provides only partial derivation, with several critical components missing—most notably, the full proof of convergence, explicit treatment of higher-order motifs beyond triangles, and formal justification of how the Bethe approximation extends to this generalized setting. Moreover, the Hamiltonian and probabilistic formulation is introduced without sufficient elaboration on how the joint distribution factorizes over overlapping motifs, which is essential for defining GBP messages and regions in the region-based approximation. The abrupt cutoff in exposition—especially the incomplete equations and lack of algorithmic description or empirical validation—further limits the paper’s current impact. For a top-tier venue like ICLR, a more complete theoretical treatment, including rigorous assumptions, lemmas, and convergence proofs, would be necessary to support the strong claims made in the abstract and introduction.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Generalized Belief Propagation' vs. Standard BP
comments.quote: In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.
comments.explanation: The abstract and contributions claim a result about generalized belief propagation (GBP) on graphs with motifs converging to the *global* optimum of the Bethe free energy, building on Koehler (2019), who studied standard BP on loopy graphs with pairwise interactions. The concern is whether the extension from standard BP on pairwise models to GBP on higher-order motifs is adequately justified or if the authors are implicitly assuming that the convergence properties carry over without additional proof. In Section 3, the authors derive the Bethe free energy for the triangle-interaction model and show GBP fixed points correspond to its stationary points. In Section 4, they adapt Koehler's monotonicity argument using a function φ. However, the update function φ used in Lemma 2 appears to be from a pairwise model—specifically f(x) = tanh(h + Σ tanh⁻¹(x_i x_j))—which contradicts the focus on triangle motifs. The correct update should involve three-spin terms or pairwise message products from the same triangle. While the paper later introduces ν_{i→jk} and λ'_{i→jk}, the actual form of φ for triangle motifs is never explicitly stated or proven to maintain monotonicity and concavity. Thus, the proof of Lemma 2 and Theorem 1 relies on unverified assumptions about φ. This constitutes a critical gap in justification for the main theoretical claim.

comments.title: Underspecified Message Initialization and Convergence Domain
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages ν_{i→jk}(0) = 1 and there is at least one fixed point in [x*,1)^n.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 assumes messages are initialized to ν_{i→jk}(0) = 1 and that a fixed point exists in [x*,1)^n. However, x* is defined in the proof of Lemma 2 as the maximum of x*_{ij}, where x*_{ij} is the largest root of an undefined function g(x) in (0,1). The function g(x) depends on weights w_i, w_j that are introduced without context or relation to model parameters (e.g., J_{ijk}, h_i). Consequently, the domain [x*,1)^n is underspecified: a reader cannot determine x* a priori or verify whether the condition holds for a given model. Additionally, initializing ν_{i→jk} = 1 implies λ'_{i→jk} → ∞, which may lead to numerical instability. While such choices may be justified by prior work (e.g., Koehler), the paper fails to clarify or defend them. This underspecification limits the practical applicability and verifiability of the convergence result.

comments.explanation: The derivation from the original Bethe free energy G_Bethe to the dual expression involving F_{ijk}(λ) is presented as a simple 'rearranging terms', but it skips non-trivial steps involving variational reparameterization and convex duality. F_{ijk}(λ) resembles a log-partition function for a three-node clique under a transformed measure, but the elimination of beliefs P_i and P_{ijk} in favor of dual variables λ'_{i→jk} is not shown. Standard derivations (e.g., Wainwright & Jordan, Yedidia et al.) rely on Legendre transforms or Lagrange multipliers with exponential family structure. Here, the transformation is asserted without derivation, citation, or explanation of how constraints are dualized. The subsequent corollary and Lemma 1 depend on this dual form, making the omission problematic. While the final expression may be correct, its lack of justification undermines theoretical rigor.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption in dual Bethe free energy derivation
comments.quote: By rearranging terms, we have [expression for G^*(ν)].
comments.explanation: This issue overlaps significantly with 'Missing Justification for Dual Bethe Free Energy Expression'. The step from Equation (45) to (48) involves substituting beliefs in terms of ν_{i→jk} = tanh(λ'_{i→jk}), which requires verifying that this reparameterization captures the full degree of freedom under marginalization constraints. Such justifications typically appear via cavity methods or duality in variational inference (e.g., Yedidia et al. 2003). The paper provides no derivation or citation to established frameworks (e.g., region graphs or Kikuchi approximations), making the step appear hand-waved. Although the final form is standard, the absence of justification is a weakness. Merged into the stronger-formulated 'Missing Justification' issue above.

comments.title: Missing factor in entropy approximation for higher-order Bethe
comments.quote: Using Equation (17), we get the Bethe approximation to the entropy as $$ S_{\text{Bethe}} = - \sum_i (q_i - 1) \sum_{x_i} P_i(x_i) \log P_i(x_i) + \sum_{(i,j,k)} \sum_{x_i,x_j,x_k} P_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k) \log P_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k) $$
comments.explanation: The coefficient (q_i - 1) for node entropy correction is unjustified. In standard generalized BP (e.g., Yoon et al. 2011), the correction factor should be a pseudo-count c_i derived from the region graph construction, not necessarily q_i - 1 (the number of incident triangles minus one). For instance, overlapping motifs require more nuanced counting to avoid over- or undercounting entropy contributions. The paper assumes a tree-like hypergraph structure which might justify this choice, but it is not stated. Without justification, this appears as an arbitrary or incorrect coefficient, undermining the validity of the Bethe free energy formulation. This issue is distinct and significant enough to retain.

comments.title: Misleading variable scope in Lagrangian multiplier definition
comments.quote: Let λ_{i→jk}(x_i) be a multiplier that enforces the marginalization constraint ∑_{x_j,x_k} P_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k) = P_i(x_i), and λ_i be a multiplier that enforces the normalization of P_i(x_i).
comments.explanation: The multiplier λ_{i→jk}(x_i) is indexed by the spin value x_i, suggesting it is a function over spin states. However, in Equation (21), it collapses to a scalar λ'_{i→jk}, implying that λ_{i→jk}(x_i) = λ'_{i→jk} x_i (affine in x_i). This functional form is standard in exponential families but is never stated. Omitting this step misleads readers about how constraints are enforced in the variational derivation. Experts may infer it from context (e.g., Yedidia et al.), but the paper should clarify this reparameterization to maintain accessibility and rigor.

comments.title: Corollary 1 not properly stated due to missing expression
comments.quote:
**Corollary 1.** _The dual Bethe free energy in terms of hyper-edge messages is_

_where_
comments.explanation: Corollary 1 is presented without any formula, breaking the logical flow just before Section 4. Given Lemma 1 and the definitions ν_{i→jk} = tanh(λ'_{i→jk}) and θ_{ijk} = tanh(J_{ijk}), one would expect an explicit expression for G^*_{Bethe}(ν) involving these quantities, such as a sum over cliques of log-normalizers. The omission is likely due to OCR or formatting error, but as presented, it prevents readers from verifying the connection between the dual variables and the free energy. This undermines confidence in the entire dual formulation and weakens the foundation of the convergence analysis in Section 4.

comments.title: Gradient inequality in Lemma 3 lacks connection to actual dual free energy gradient
comments.quote: **Lemma 3.** _If ν ∈ Spre then ∇G^*_{Bethe}(ν) ≤ 0 and if ν ∈ Spost then ∇G^*_{Bethe}(ν) ≥ 0
comments.explanation: The lemma makes claims about the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy, but no explicit form of G^*_{Bethe}(ν) is ever provided (especially due to the missing Corollary 1). The proof refers to a phantom Equation (66) and fails to show how ∇G^*_{Bethe} relates to the message update function φ. In standard GBP, ∇G^* ≈ ν - φ(ν), so sign conditions follow from whether ν ≤ φ(ν). This identification is not made here. Without demonstrating this link, the lemma remains unjustified and disconnects the optimization landscape from the algorithm dynamics. This weakens the theoretical justification for Theorem 1.

comments.title: Misleading domain in Lemma 2 involving improper summation notation
comments.quote: **Lemma 2.** _Suppose that f(x) = tanh(h + ∑_{(i,j)} tanh⁻¹(x_i x_j)) for any h ≥ 0. Then f is a concave monotone function on the domain [x*,1)^n._
comments.explanation: The summation index (i,j) and use of x_i x_j suggest pairwise interactions, but the model is defined solely on triangle motifs with no pairwise terms. This contradicts the paper's own setup. The intended update likely involves messages from triangle neighbors, e.g., tanh⁻¹(ν_{m→in} ν_{n→im}) summed over triangles incident to i excluding the target. The use of (i,j) evokes standard BP, not GBP on triangles. While OCR artifacts ('[]') may contribute, the mismatch suggests either a fundamental inconsistency or a poorly adapted proof. This raises serious doubts about whether Lemma 2 applies to the actual model, thereby undermining the proof of Theorem 1.

Although the result may be standard under ferromagnetic assumptions, the absence of derivation prevents verification. This is likely due to OCR failure, but as presented, it severely weakens the credibility of Lemma 2 and, by extension, Theorem 1.

comments.title: Overstatement of Generality in Abstract and Introduction
comments.quote: In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims generality over 'graphs with motifs', but the analysis is restricted to models with triangle interactions and no pairwise terms. The proof techniques depend on properties of the triangle-specific update rule and may not extend to arbitrary motifs (e.g., squares, cliques). While the authors suggest the result can be extended, the current proof does not support such generality. This overstatement could mislead readers about the scope of the contribution. However, this issue is logically distinct and mild.

comments.title: Overstatement of convergence guarantee in Theorem 1
comments.quote: The messages converge to a fixed point ν^* of the generalized BP equations such that for any other fixed point μ, μ_{i→jk} ≤ ν^*_{i→jk}. Furthermore G_Bethe(ν^*) ≥ G_Bethe(μ) for any other fixed point μ.
comments.explanation: The paper claims convergence to the *global maximum* of the Bethe free energy, but the proof only establishes that ν^* dominates all fixed points in a partial order and maximizes G_Bethe among fixed points. It does not rule out the existence of other fixed points outside [x*,1)^n with higher free energy. Also, no proof ensures all fixed points are in this region. The argument relies on monotonicity from initialization at 1, but global optimality requires convexity or uniqueness not proven here. The claim should be softened to convergence to the *largest fixed point* under the order, not global maximum. This moderate overstatement risks misrepresenting the result’s strength.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of ν ∈ S_post in Theorem 1 proof
comments.quote: Finally, consider any other point ν ∈ S_post, by convexity of S_post, we know that the line segment from ν to ν^* is entirely contained in S_post. By Lemma 3, we see that for any point x on this interpolating line segment that ∇G_Bethe(ν) · (ν^* − ν) ≥ 0
comments.explanation: The proof attempts to show G(ν) ≤ G(ν^*) by integrating the gradient along the segment from ν to ν^*. It uses Lemma 3, which states ∇G ≥ 0 on S_post. But ∇G ≥ 0 component-wise does not imply ∇G · (ν^* − ν) ≥ 0 unless ν^* ≥ ν, i.e., ν ≤ ν^*. However, ν ∈ S_post only means ν ≥ φ(ν), and ν^* is a fixed point; the partial order between them is not established. The proof assumes ν ≤ ν^* without justification. This flaw invalidates the optimality argument. This is a critical logical gap in the main proof and is the most serious issue identified.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: This paper investigates the convergence properties of generalized belief propagation (GBP) on graphs with motifs—such as triangles and higher-order cliques—for ferromagnetic Ising models, extending prior theoretical results that have largely focused on locally tree-like or simple loopy graphs. The work is well-motivated, clearly situated within the relevant literature, and addresses an important gap in the theoretical understanding of message-passing algorithms in structured, non-tree-like networks arising in real-world domains. The writing is generally clear, and the progression from problem motivation to technical setup is logical. A key strength lies in its principled extension of convergence guarantees from standard belief propagation (as established by Koehler, 2019) to the more complex setting of generalized BP on hyper-graphs with motifs. However, the most significant issue is the incomplete development of the theoretical argument: while the paper claims convergence of GBP to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy under all-ones initialization, it provides only partial derivation, with several critical components missing—most notably, the full proof of convergence, explicit treatment of higher-order motifs beyond triangles, and formal justification of how the Bethe approximation extends to this generalized setting. Moreover, the Hamiltonian and probabilistic formulation is introduced without sufficient elaboration on how the joint distribution factorizes over overlapping motifs, which is essential for defining GBP messages and regions in the region-based approximation. The abrupt cutoff in exposition—especially the incomplete equations and lack of algorithmic description or empirical validation—further limits the paper’s current impact. For a top-tier venue like ICLR, a more complete theoretical treatment, including rigorous assumptions, lemmas, and convergence proofs, would be necessary to support the strong claims made in the abstract and introduction.

comments.quote:
Consider the Ising model with arbitrary order of interactions among vertices in each motif on a hyper-graph. Let _Ml_ ( _i_ ) denote a cluster of size _l_ attached to vertex _i_ , where vertices _j_ 1 _, j_ 2 _, . . . , jl−_ 1 together with _i_ form the motif. And let _X_ denote the random variable of spin configurations, the Hamiltonian of the model is

where the first sum corresponds to the external fields at each vertex, the second sum corresponds to the pairwise interactions on simple edges, the third sum corresponds to the higher order interactions among spins in triangles, the fourth sum corresponds to the higher order interactions among spins in squares, and so on.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the description suggests a general Hamiltonian with interactions of arbitrary order (edges, triangles, squares, etc.), but the equation itself is not displayed—only its components are described in prose. This creates ambiguity: is this meant to be a placeholder for a formal expression, or was the equation lost due to OCR? However, later in the text, the authors specify that they focus on models with only external fields and triangle interactions, and give a concrete form for that case (a sum over triangles with J_ijk X_i X_j X_k). Given this, and the stated intent to simplify, the omission of the full general Hamiltonian equation seems intentional rather than an error. The concern is resolved by context: they are setting up generality but immediately specialize. Still, the lack of even a schematic formula (e.g., H = -∑ h_i X_i - ∑ J_ij X_i X_j - ∑ J_ijk X_i X_j X_k - ...) could mislead a careful reader into thinking a definition is missing. This is a mild clarity issue in presentation, not a technical flaw.

comments.title: Potential Index Misalignment in Motif Notation M₃(i)
comments.quote: where ( _i, j, k_ ) is a triangle, which can also be denoted as _M_ 3( _i_ ), _M_ 3( _j_ ), or _M_ 3( _k_ ).
comments.explanation: The notation M₃(i) suggests a triangle motif 'attached to' vertex i, but a triangle involves three vertices. So, denoting the same triangle as M₃(i), M₃(j), and M₃(k) implies that M₃(i) does not uniquely identify a specific triangle—it is used to mean 'the triangle incident to i that includes j and k'. This could be confusing if taken to imply that M₃(i) is a function returning a unique cluster. However, in the context of message-passing algorithms on hypergraphs, it is standard to index hyperedges (motifs) per incident node for message routing (e.g., sending a message from the triangle to node i). The authors later define messages like μ_{M₃(i)} → i, which confirms that M₃(i) is being used to refer to a motif from the perspective of vertex i. This is consistent with generalized BP conventions (e.g., Yoon et al.). Thus, while slightly ambiguous at first, the notation aligns with standard usage in the literature. No real error exists.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Generalized Belief Propagation' vs. Standard BP
comments.quote: In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.
comments.explanation: The abstract and contributions claim a result about generalized belief propagation (GBP) on graphs with motifs converging to the *global* optimum of the Bethe free energy, building on Koehler (2019), who studied standard BP on loopy graphs with pairwise interactions. The concern is whether the extension from standard BP on pairwise models to GBP on higher-order motifs is adequately justified or if the authors are implicitly assuming that the convergence properties carry over without additional proof. However, in Section 3, the authors do derive the Bethe free energy for the triangle-interaction model and show that the GBP fixed points correspond to its stationary points, which is crucial. Then in Section 4, they adapt Koehler's argument using monotonicity and concavity of the update function φ. The function f(x) = tanh(h + Σ tanh⁻¹(x_i x_j)) is indeed reminiscent of standard BP, but in the triangle case, the update rule should involve pairwise products or three-spin terms. Yet the proof of Lemma 2 uses a form that appears more aligned with pairwise interactions. This raises concern: is the update function φ correctly adapted to triangle motifs? Later, in Equation (12) and surrounding text, they define ν_{i→jk} = tanh(λ_{M₃(i)}), with λ derived from belief updates involving triangle energies. But the specific form of φ is not given, only referred to symbolically. The proof of Lemma 2 assumes a form that may not reflect the actual triangle-based message update. This is a real gap: the argument in Lemma 2 and Theorem 1 depends on monotonicity and concavity of φ, but the function φ for triangle motifs is more complex than tanh(h + Σ tanh⁻¹(x_i x_j)). Without explicitly stating and justifying the form of φ for the triangle case, the proof's applicability is undersupported. This weakens the main claim.

comments.title: Missing Justification for Dual Bethe Free Energy Expression
comments.quote:
Recall the Bethe free energy

By rearranging terms, we have

_Fijk_ ( _λ_ ) = E[ _JijkXiXjXk_ + _λ[′] i→jk[X][i]_[+] _[ λ][′] j→ik[X][j]_[+] _[ λ][′] k→ij[X][k]_[] +] _[ H]_[(] _[X][i][, X][j][, X][k]_[)] (46)
comments.explanation: The derivation from the original Bethe free energy to the expression involving F_{ijk}(λ) is presented as a simple 'rearranging terms', but this skips non-trivial algebraic and variational steps. The F_{ijk}(λ) term appears to be a log-partition-like function for the three-node clique, but its derivation from the entropy and energy terms in G_Bethe is not shown. In standard treatments (e.g., Wainwright & Jordan), such dual forms arise via convex duality or Legendre transforms. Here, the authors jump to a form without showing how the beliefs P_i and P_{ijk} are eliminated in favor of the λ' multipliers. While this may be standard for experts, the paper does not cite a source or provide a derivation sketch. Given that the subsequent corollary (involving θ_{ijk} = tanh(J_{ijk})) depends on this form, the lack of justification is problematic. The result may be correct (inspired by Yedidia et al. and Koehler), but the omission makes it hard to verify. This is especially important since the main convergence result rests on properties of this dual energy.

comments.title: Overstatement of Generality in Abstract and Introduction
comments.quote: In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.
comments.explanation: The claim is broad: 'graphs with motifs' and 'generalized belief propagation'. However, in Section 2, the authors restrict to Ising models with only external fields and triangle interactions. Later, in the proof of Theorem 1, they assume the existence of a fixed point in [x*,1)^n and rely on properties of the tanh-based update function that may not hold for general motifs (e.g., squares, cliques). The core analysis appears specific to triangle-based models with ferromagnetic couplings. While they mention the results can be extended, the current proof does not cover arbitrary motifs. Thus, the abstract and contribution paragraph overstate the scope. A careful reader might infer broader applicability than currently proven. This is common in ML papers, but still a mild overclaim.

comments.title: Underspecified Message Initialization and Convergence Domain
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi[(0)] _→jk_[=][1] and there is at least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] .
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 assumes that initialization is ν_{i→jk}(0) = 1 for all hyperedges, and that there exists a fixed point in [x*,1)^n. However, x* is defined in the proof of Lemma 2 as the maximum over (i,j) of x*_{ij}, where x*_{ij} is the largest root of g(x) in (0,1). But g(x) is not fully defined in the current passage—it depends on weights w_i, w_j that are introduced without context. The set [x*,1)^n is thus underspecified: what determines x*? How does it relate to model parameters (J_{ijk}, h_i)? The convergence is only guaranteed to a fixed point in this domain, but without characterizing x*, the result is not actionable. A reader cannot determine a priori whether the condition holds. Moreover, initializing messages to 1 corresponds to tanh(λ) = 1, i.e., λ→∞, which may not be numerically stable. While Koehler (2019) may justify this, the current paper does not clarify these choices. This is a moderate underspecification of conditions.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of Hamiltonian in Equation (3) vs. later usage
comments.quote: the Hamiltonian of the model is $$ -H(X) = \sum_i h_i X_i + \sum_{(i,j)} J_{ij} X_i X_j + \sum_{(i,j,k)} J_{ijk} X_i X_j X_k + \sum_{(i,j,k,l)} J_{ijkl} X_i X_j X_k X_l + \cdots $$
comments.explanation: The full Hamiltonian includes pairwise interactions $ J_{ij} $, but later in Section 2 and in the key assumptions, the paper states it focuses on models with only external fields and triangle interactions. This contradicts the general form unless $ J_{ij} = 0 $ is explicitly assumed. However, the paper does say 'for simplicity, we consider Ising models with only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles.' So this is not an outright error, but the initial equation (3) should be revised or clarified to reflect that $ J_{ij} = 0 $ for non-motif edges, or the sum over $ (i,j) $ should be omitted. As written, a reader might incorrectly believe pairwise interactions are retained, which would affect the Bethe free energy derivation. The claim is later self-consistent, but the opening presentation is misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in Equation (5): $ M_3(i) $ not clearly distinguished as triangle motif
comments.quote: where $ M_l(i) $ denote a cluster of size $ l $ attached to vertex $ i $
comments.explanation: While the definition of $ M_l(i) $ is introduced, the notation $ M_3(i) $ is used without initially clarifying that it refers specifically to the triangle motif including $ i $. In Fig. 1 and context it becomes clear, but in Equation (5), $ M_3(i) $ appears as though it is a unique object, whereas in reality each triangle (i,j,k) is associated with three such notations: $ M_3(i) $, $ M_3(j) $, $ M_3(k) $. This could confuse a reader about whether $ M_3(i) $ uniquely identifies a single motif or depends on context. The ambiguity is resolved later when $ (i,j,k) $ and $ \nu_{i\to jk} $ notation are used, so this is not a fatal flaw, but it momentarily obscures the symmetry of the representation.

comments.title: Misleading variable scope in Lagrangian multiplier definition
comments.quote: Let $ \lambda_{i\to jk}(x_i) $ be a multiplier that enforces the marginalization constraint $ \sum_{x_j,x_k} P_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k) = P_i(x_i) $, and $ \lambda_i $ be a multiplier that enforces the normalization of $ P_i(x_i) $.
comments.explanation: The multiplier $ \lambda_{i\to jk}(x_i) $ is indexed by both the directed edge $ i \to jk $ and the spin value $ x_i $, suggesting it depends on $ x_i $. However, upon reaching the derivation in Equation (21), the transform leads to a scalar $ \lambda'_{i\to jk} $, which collapses the $ x_i $ dependence. This suggests that $ \lambda_{i\to jk}(x_i) $ is actually affine in $ x_i $, i.e., $ \lambda_{i\to jk}(x_i) = \lambda'_{i\to jk} x_i $, but this functional form is not stated. This is standard in BP derivations (e.g., Yedidia et al.), so experts will infer it, but omitting this makes the derivation appear to skip a critical step. The missing clarification could mislead a less experienced reader about how constraints are enforced in exponential families.

comments.title: Incorrect subscript in normalization constraint reference
comments.quote: and the equation $ \partial P_i(x_i) / \partial L $ gives:
comments.explanation: The notation $ \partial P_i(x_i) / \partial L $ is used, but this is non-standard and likely an OCR or formatting error. The correct expression should be $ \partial L / \partial P_i(x_i) = 0 $. The numerator and denominator are reversed, which would be nonsensical mathematically. Given the surrounding LaTeX fragment '[= 0]', it is clear the author meant the derivative of $ L $ with respect to $ P_i(x_i) $. Other similar terms (like $ \partial L / \partial P_{ijk} $) appear correctly. This is likely an OCR misread of '\partial L / \partial P_i', and for this reason, it's not a major issue — but as written, it would confuse a careful reader.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption in dual Bethe free energy derivation
comments.quote: By rearranging terms, we have [expression for $ G^*(\nu) $].
comments.explanation: The step from Equation (45) to (48) in the dual Bethe free energy derivation is described as 'by rearranging terms,' but it involves substituting expressions for beliefs in terms of $ \nu_{i\to jk} = \tanh(\lambda'_{i\to jk}) $, and computing expectations under a reparameterized model. This is non-trivial and requires verifying that the parameterization via hyper-edge messages captures all degrees of freedom. In Yedidia et al. (2003) and related works, this is justified via Legendre duality or cavity arguments. Here, the justification is absent, and Lemma 1 is stated almost axiomatically. While the final expression is standard, the lack of derivation or citation makes this step appear hand-wavy. The correctness can be recovered by context and expert knowledge, but the paper should either cite Yoon et al. (2011) more explicitly or sketch the reparameterization.

comments.title: Missing factor in entropy approximation for higher-order Bethe
comments.quote: Using Equation (17), we get the Bethe approximation to the entropy as $$ S_{\text{Bethe}} = - \sum_i (q_i - 1) \sum_{x_i} P_i(x_i) \log P_i(x_i) + \sum_{(i,j,k)} \sum_{x_i,x_j,x_k} P_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k) \log P_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k) $$
comments.explanation: In standard Bethe approximation for hypergraphs, the entropy correction should account for overcounting of node entropies when motifs overlap. The coefficient $ (q_i - 1) $ is commonly used in tree-like hypergraphs to adjust for the fact that each node appears in $ q_i $ motifs, and its entropy is counted multiple times. However, in typical generalized BP formulations (e.g., Yoon et al. 2011), the correct coefficient for a node in the Bethe entropy is its pseudo-count $ c_i $, which may not equal $ q_i - 1 $. For example, in a hypergraph with overlapping triangles, $ q_i $ counts the number of triangle motifs incident to $ i $, but $ q_i - 1 $ is not a standard correction factor. The correct form should be $ -\sum_i (1 - \delta_i) H_i + \sum_{\alpha} H_\alpha $, where $ \delta_i $ is a counting number depending on the region graph construction. The paper assumes a specific topological structure (tree-like with triangles only) which may justify $ q_i - 1 $, but this is not explained. Without justification, this choice appears arbitrary or incorrect.

comments.title: Overstatement of convergence guarantee in Theorem 1
comments.quote:
The messages converge to a fixed point $ 
u^* $ of the generalized BP equations such that for any other fixed point $ \mu $, $ \mu_{i	o jk} \leq 
u^*_{i	o jk} $. Furthermore $ G_{	ext{Bethe}}(
u^*) \geq G_{	ext{Bethe}}(\mu) $ for any other fixed point $ \mu $.
comments.explanation: The theorem claims the algorithm converges to the *global maximum* of the Bethe free energy, but the proof only shows that the limit $ \nu^* $ dominates all other fixed points component-wise and that $ G_{\text{Bethe}} $ is maximized among fixed points. However, it does not prove that all fixed points lie in $ [x^*,1)^n $ or that $ S_{\text{post}} $ contains only one fixed point. In principle, a fixed point outside this region could have higher Bethe free energy. The argument relies on monotonicity and initialization at 1, which Koehler uses for standard Ising models, but extending this to higher-order motifs requires stronger assumptions (e.g., convexity of energy landscape) not verified here. While plausible under ferromagnetic assumption and tree-like initialization, the claim exceeds what’s proven. progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a theoretically significant gap in graphical model inference by extending the convergence guarantees of the belief propagation (BP) algorithm from simple graphs to those featuring complex motifs or higher-order interactions. The motivation is well-articulated, successfully framing the transition from standard pairwise Ising models to more complex network structures that better reflect real-world topology. However, the manuscript suffers from inconsistent technical precision and clarity issues that hinder its impact. While the authors outline a clear roadmap—building directly upon the formalisms of Yedidia et al. (2003) and Koehler (2019)—the transition between the problem statement and the formal setup in Section 2 is abrupt. Specifically, the definition of the Hamiltonian for motifs (Equation 2) is mathematically dense but lacks sufficient commentary to guide the reader through the transition from pairwise to higher-order interactions. To elevate the quality of the submission, the authors must address these notational hurdles, ensure consistent terminology (e.g., distinguishing clearly between edges, cliques, and general motifs), and provide a more rigorous justification for why their specific convergence guarantees under "all-one" initialization hold for more complex motif geometries beyond simple triangles.

comments.title: Missing definition of Hamiltonian and associated equations
comments.quote: the joint distribution of Ising model is given by...
comments.explanation: Multiple key equations are missing or poorly defined. The introductory definition of the Hamiltonian is absent, the effective field 'lambda' equation is ambiguous due to potential OCR issues and unclear summation indices, and the Bethe Free Energy expression contains sign inconsistencies and notation that conflates probability distributions with summation indices. These gaps impede verification of the BP fixed points and the variational free energy derivation.

comments.quote: generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy
comments.explanation: The authors claim that 'all-one' message initialization leads to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy. This relies on a generalization of Koehler (2019) from pairwise graphs to hypergraphs with triangle motifs. The claim is significant and non-trivial; the provided proof sketch is insufficient to bridge the gap between simple edges and triangle-based interactions, leaving the justification for this specific initialization as a global optimum incomplete.

comments.quote: generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy
comments.explanation: The authors define the initialization as 'all one messages' (which corresponds to neutral/zero field). While this is a common trick for ferro-magnets to reach the global maximum, the claim of converging to the 'global optimum' (maximum) is quite strong. The proof in Theorem 1 relies on the Bethe free energy being well-behaved and the sequence being monotonic. Given that Bethe free energy on loopy graphs can have multiple local maxima, the link between the 'all one' initialization and the specific global maximum relies on the properties of the Bethe landscape for the Ising model, which is a significant claim. While Koehler (2019) is cited for the simple graph case, generalizing this to motif-based hypergraphs is non-trivial and the proof sketch for this extension is brief.

However, the equation provided appears to omit the sum over the spin configurations for the entropy term of the P_i marginals, or at least uses notation that conflates the probability distribution with the summation index, which is inconsistent with the entropy expression provided in the Summary section: 'sum_i (1-q_i) sum P_i ln P_i'. If P_i denotes the probability distribution (a vector), then 'sum P_i ln P_i' is standard, but the presentation in the paper is ambiguous regarding whether it is a summation over x_i. More importantly, the average energy U(P) in (16) and the entropy term S(P) combined in (19) should lead to the Bethe free energy, but the signs and factors relative to the Hamiltonian H(X) (where beta is reciprocal temperature) are occasionally inconsistent with the standard definition of the variational free energy.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript investigates the convergence of Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) on graphs with motifs for ferromagnetic Ising models, a topic of significant interest given the limitations of existing theory on simple loopy graphs. While the objective to extend Koehler's (2019) convergence results to motif-based structures is valuable, the current quality of the submission is compromised by substantial presentation deficiencies and ambiguities that hinder evaluation. The writing exhibits grammatical inconsistencies ("One of the ways are"), informal tone ("Basically"), and LaTeX rendering errors that disrupt the flow, alongside critical citation mistakes (e.g., "Newman Newman"). The most significant technical issue lies in the formulation of the model: the paper claims to address "ferromagnetic" models but defines the Hamiltonian with "higher order interactions" in motifs without explicitly specifying the sign constraints or coupling conditions required to generalize the ferromagnetic property to these multi-vertex clusters; without this definition, the core assumption enabling the convergence proof is unclear. Additionally, there is a lack of precision regarding the initialization, which is described as "all one messages" in the contribution but vaguely as a "certain initialization" in the abstract, and the manuscript must rigorously demonstrate how the presence of motifs alters the landscape of the Bethe free energy compared to simple graphs, ensuring that the generalization is non-trivial and technically sound.

Furthermore, immediately following the definition of λ′, the authors state 'we find that at a critical point of the Lagrangian that' but omit the resulting equation. This duplication and missing intermediate step obscure the crucial algebraic connection between the Lagrange multipliers and the message updates (ν = tanh(λ′)). While using differences of multipliers to isolate effective fields is standard in variational BP derivations, the copy-paste error and skipped equation break the local derivation and prevent verification of how marginalization constraints translate into the hyper-edge consistency equations used later.

This formulation obscures the domain of f, does not align with the log-odds additivity of independent spins, and complicates verifying the claimed concavity and monotonicity on [x^*, 1)^n. comments.title: Non-standard definition of ferromagnetic Ising model (Paragraph 16)
comments.quote: An Ising model is ferromagnetic if Jijk ≥_ 0 _for all triangle motifs_ ( _i, j, k_ ) _and hi ≥_ 0 _for all i._
comments.explanation: Standard statistical physics literature defines a ferromagnetic model solely by non-negative interaction strengths (J ≥ 0), with external fields h_i typically unconstrained. The authors restrict h_i ≥ 0 to ensure effective fields remain non-negative, which is necessary for their convergence proof (messages initialized to 1 will then decrease monotonically). While functionally justified for their specific theorem, labeling this restricted class as the definition of 'ferromagnetic' is a terminology stretch that could mislead readers into thinking the model cannot handle arbitrary external fields. It remains a minor framing issue.

A careful reader cannot verify why the gradient is non-positive on S_pre and non-negative on S_post. Furthermore, the non-negativity of the dot product relies on the unstated fact that ν* - ν ≥ 0 component-wise. The proof assumes this without justification, which is essential for concluding that ν* is a global maximum over S_post. The missing component-wise inequality and functional switch introduce ambiguity in the convergence claim.

comments.quote: Suppose that f(x) = tanh(h + \sum_{(i,j)} \tanh^{-1}(x_i x_j)) for any h ≥ 0. Then f is a concave monotone function on the domain [x^*, 1)^n.

The expression $\sum_{(i,j)} \tanh^{-1}(x_i x_j)$ inside the outer $\tanh$ is non-standard and structurally ambiguous in this context. If $x$ represents messages, multiplying them inside the atanh does not correspond to the log-odds additivity of independent spins. It is unclear whether this is a deliberate bounding function borrowed from pairwise literature (e.g., Koehler 2019), a typo for $\sum \tanh^{-1}(x_j)$, or a projection not explained in the missing surrounding equations. Without clarification, verifying the claimed concavity and monotonicity on $[x^*, 1)^n$ is difficult, and it may hinder reproducibility of the proof step.

Even assuming [] is an OCR artifact for ∑, the argument x_i x_j inside the inverse hyperbolic tangent does not align with standard message-passing formulations on hyper-graphs with motifs, where updates depend on neighbor-to-node messages or correlations. progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: Based on the provided excerpt, the paper attempts to extend known convergence results for belief propagation on loopy pairwise graphs to generalized belief propagation on graphs with higher-order motifs (e.g., triangles). However, several issues detract from the paper's clarity and quality. First, the writing contains typographical errors (e.g., "PROPAGA TION" in the title, redundant "Under review" headers) and occasionally digresses into tangential discussions (e.g., the detailed critique of Gibbs sampling) that do not directly motivate the contribution. The central claim—that generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs—is a very strong one, yet the excerpt provides no precise theorem statement, no sketch of the proof strategy, and no discussion of how the generalization from pairwise edges to motifs is achieved theoretically. The paper also does not clarify how “generalized belief propagation” is defined on motifs or how the Bethe free energy is formulated in this setting. Without these critical details, the reader cannot assess the novelty or correctness of the claimed result. Overall, while the topic is relevant and the extension to motifs is potentially valuable, the paper would benefit from a more focused introduction, a clear statement of contributions and assumptions, and preliminary technical justification to support its ambitious convergence claim.

The notation is ambiguous: the sum over (i,j) is not defined, and the domain is a vector but the function outputs a scalar, inconsistent with the per-message update context. The authors do not explain how this lemma applies to triangle motifs, creating a fundamental gap in the proof of Theorem 1.

The passage does not provide a separate lemma for the triangle case, nor does it explain how the pairwise lemma extends.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by extending the convergence guarantees of Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) to graphs with non-trivial motifs, such as triangles and higher-order cliques, addressing a critical gap between theoretical results for tree-like structures and the complex topologies of real-world networks. The introduction is well-structured and clearly articulates the motivation, correctly situating the work within the context of the Ising model and the limitations of standard MCMC or variational methods. However, a significant challenge for the manuscript is the technical verification of the claimed result: that GBP converges to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy under an all-one initialization. The definitions provided for the Ising model with motifs are sound, but the critical link between these motif-based interactions and the stability properties of GBP messages remains to be demonstrated in the main body of the paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous domain condition relative to initialization
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi_[(0)] →jk [=][1] and there is at] least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n]
comments.explanation:
Theorem 1 assumes the initialization is $
u_i^{(0)} = 1$ (implied by the strong formatting around `=1`). However, it requires the existence of a fixed point 'in' the domain $[x^*, 1)$. If $x^*$ is strictly less than 1, then the initialization point 1 lies outside this domain. In standard optimization or dynamics context, one usually considers the fixed point to be a limit point of the trajectory. If 1 is a fixed point (which is often the case for the 'all +1' configuration in ferromagnetic Ising models when initialized to 1), then the domain condition excludes the trivial fixed point or treats it as a boundary condition not covered by the statement. If $x^* = 1$, the interval is empty. This suggests a potential inconsistency in the domain definition or a slight imprecision in the phrasing regarding boundary points.
